result:
On ordinary mornings, the world rehearses miracles under a thin coat of silence. Streetlights blink out, coffee machines cough, and windows lift their eyelids. In that hinge of day, attention feels like a small prayer: not for answers, but for presence. I try to practice noticing the way steam threads itself through breath, how the neighbor’s dog edits the morning with an exclamation.

The internet trains us to skim, to let days bead up and roll off the waxed surface of our attention. But the grain of a table remembers storms inside its rings, and the bus seat remembers legible maps of strangers’ weight. If you wait long enough, even a crosswalk will confess choreography: the red hand’s stern sermon, the white figure stepping out like a rumor made official.

Lately I keep a pocket of questions where my phone used to live. What will the wind decide about the laundry? Which shadow arrives first when the sun rounds the chimney? If a friend texts a single ellipsis, how many rooms does it open inside me? I don’t pretend this practice solves anything. It doesn’t fix the news or cure the moon’s exhaustion. It only loosens the knot between before and after, so the present can widen a little, like a sleeve you roll once more before lifting something heavy, discovering it’s lighter than you feared, but still worth two hands, still worth carrying carefully, past the hedges, along the curb, into the bright, unpunctuated day, where the commas breathe.

content.text:
On ordinary mornings, the world rehearses miracles under a thin coat of silence. Streetlights blink out, coffee machines cough, and windows lift their eyelids. In that hinge of day, attention feels like a small prayer: not for answers, but for presence. I try to practice noticing the way steam threads itself through breath, how the neighbor’s dog edits the morning with an exclamation.

The internet trains us to skim, to let days bead up and roll off the waxed surface of our attention. But the grain of a table remembers storms inside its rings, and the bus seat remembers legible maps of strangers’ weight. If you wait long enough, even a crosswalk will confess choreography: the red hand’s stern sermon, the white figure stepping out like a rumor made official.

Lately I keep a pocket of questions where my phone used to live. What will the wind decide about the laundry? Which shadow arrives first when the sun rounds the chimney? If a friend texts a single ellipsis, how many rooms does it open inside me? I don’t pretend this practice solves anything. It doesn’t fix the news or cure the moon’s exhaustion. It only loosens the knot between before and after, so the present can widen a little, like a sleeve you roll once more before lifting something heavy, discovering it’s lighter than you feared, but still worth two hands, still worth carrying carefully, past the hedges, along the curb, into the bright, unpunctuated day, where the commas breathe.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
